    , axis.text.y = ggplot2::element_blank()
    , axis.ticks.y = ggplot2::element_blank()
  )

the trend of increasing detection rate with tree height demonstrated with this data aligns well with prior work using aerial point cloud data for single-tree forest inventories

let’s look at the commission rate (commission error) by tree height using the UAS-detected tree heights to determine if our ITD variable window function could be improved

ground_truth_prediction_match_ans %>%
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
  dplyr::filter(match_grp %in% c("true positive","commission")) %>% 
  # dplyr::glimpse()
  # metric cuts/bins
  dplyr::mutate(
    tree_height_m_bin = ggplot2::cut_width(pred_tree_height_m, width = 3, boundary = 0, closed = "left") %>% 
      make_bins_pretty(suffix = "m") %>% 
      forcats::fct_relabel(~ stringr::str_replace(.x, "^0-", paste0(min_tree_ht_m,"-")) )
    , is_detected = match_grp %in% c("true positive")
    , is_detected = as.numeric(is_detected)
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::count(match_grp, tree_height_m_bin) %>% 
  dplyr::group_by(tree_height_m_bin) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(pct = n/sum(n,na.rm = T)) %>% 
  dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    lab = ifelse(
      pct>0.095
      , paste0(
        scales::percent(pct,accuracy=1) 
        ,"\n("
        , scales::comma(n,accuracy=1) 
        , ")"
      )
      , ""
    )
    , match_grp = forcats::fct_rev(match_grp)
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
  # dplyr::glimpse()
  ggplot2::ggplot(
    mapping = ggplot2::aes(y = pct, x = tree_height_m_bin)
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_col(
    mapping = ggplot2::aes(fill = match_grp)
    , width = 0.6
    , color = NA, alpha = 0.9
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_text(
    mapping = ggplot2::aes(label = lab, group = match_grp, fontface = "bold", color = match_grp)
    , color = "black"
    , size = 2.5
    , position = ggplot2::position_stack(vjust = 0.5)
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_manual(values = pal_match_grp) +
  # ggplot2::scale_color_manual(values = c("black","white"), guide = "none") +
  ggplot2::scale_x_discrete(drop = F) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = seq(0,1,by=0.2)
    , labels = scales::percent
    , expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0,0.02))
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = "tree height", y = "", fill = ""
    , subtitle = "Commission rate by tree height bin"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_light()+
  ggplot2::theme(
    legend.position = "top"
    , strip.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 11, color = "black", face = "bold")
    , axis.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8, angle = 45, hjust = 1, vjust = 1)
    , axis.text.y = ggplot2::element_blank()
    , axis.ticks.y = ggplot2::element_blank()
  )

the commission error (percentage shown in the gray bar) is highest for the small trees which suggests we could potentially improve our ITD window function to decrease the number of trees detected in this height range. However, going back to the detection rate above, this same height range also had the lowest detection rate which would suggest we increase the number of trees identified in this height range. This conflict suggests that the trees in this height range are likely difficult to detect for the study area using the data and ITD methods used in this study. For example, they may be subordinate to directly adjacent taller trees with the crowns interlocking, so they are often grouped with the taller tree during the CHM-based ITD.

4.7 Physical measurement Accuracy Results

let’s table the tree-level aggregated measurement error

agg_ground_truth_match_ans %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    ref_trees = tp_n+fn_n
    , det_trees = tp_n+fp_n
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::select(
    # detection
    ref_trees
    , det_trees
    , tp_n
    , omission_rate,commission_rate,recall,precision,f_score
    # physical measurement
    , tidyselect::ends_with("_mean")
    , tidyselect::ends_with("_rmse")
    , tidyselect::ends_with("_mape")
  ) %>% 
  # second select to arrange tree_metric
  dplyr::select(
    # detection
    ref_trees
    , det_trees
    , tp_n
    , omission_rate,commission_rate,recall,precision,f_score
    # physical measurement
    # , c(tidyselect::contains("volume") & !tidyselect::contains("paraboloid"))
    , tidyselect::contains("area")
    , tidyselect::contains("height")
    , tidyselect::contains("diameter")
    , tidyselect::contains("dbh")
  ) %>% 
  # names()
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(
      .cols = c(
        f_score, recall, precision, tidyselect::ends_with("_mape")
        , tidyselect::ends_with("_rate")
      )
      , .fn = ~ scales::percent(.x, accuracy = 1)
    )
    , dplyr::across(
      .cols = c(tidyselect::ends_with("_mean"))
      , .fn = ~ scales::comma(.x, accuracy = 0.01)
    )
    , dplyr::across(
      .cols = c(tidyselect::ends_with("_rmse"))
      , .fn = ~ scales::comma(.x, accuracy = 0.01)
    )
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::select(
    !tidyselect::contains("_area_")
    & !tidyselect::contains("diff_diameter_")
    & !tidyselect::ends_with("_trees")
    # & !tidyselect::ends_with("_n")
    & !tidyselect::ends_with("_rate")
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::select(
    -c(
      recall,precision,f_score
    )
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(data = "site-wide") %>% 
  dplyr::relocate(data) %>% 
  # ncol()
  # dplyr::glimpse()
  kableExtra::kbl(
    caption = "Physical measurement Accuracy"
    , col.names = c(
      "data"
      , "TP predictions"
      , rep(c("ME","RMSE","MAPE"), times = 2)
    )
    , escape = F
    # , digits = 2
  ) %>% 
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 11.5) %>% 
  kableExtra::add_header_above(c(
    " "=2
    # , "Detection" = 3
    # , "Area" = 3
    , "Height (m)" = 3
    , "DBH (cm)" = 3
  )) %>% 
  kableExtra::column_spec(seq(2,8,by=3), border_right = TRUE, include_thead = TRUE)
Table 4.5: Physical measurement Accuracy
Height (m)
DBH (cm)
data TP predictions ME RMSE MAPE ME RMSE MAPE
site-wide 956 -0.26 0.49 7% 10.05 12.73 95%

UAS-measured heights are highly accurate but those DBH measurements do not align very well. perhaps this is due to the fact that DBH wasn’t actually measured in the field. instead, DRC (diameter at root crown) was measured and then we converted DRC to DBH using the Chojnacky & Rogers (1999) model…therefore, it’s probably best not to view these DBH metrics as measurements of “error” but as measurements of “comparison”

and plot predicted versus reference values for correct predictions (TP)

####################################
# area scatter
####################################
dta_temp <-
  ground_truth_prediction_match_ans %>% 
  dplyr::filter(match_grp=="true positive") %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
  dplyr::bind_rows() %>% 
  dplyr::bind_cols(
    agg_ground_truth_match_ans %>% 
      dplyr::select(tidyselect::ends_with("_rmse") , tidyselect::ends_with("_mape"), tidyselect::ends_with("_mean") )
  )
# plt fn temp
plt_scatter_fn <- function(
    dta
    , which_col = "dbh_cm"
    , title_lab = "DBH"
    , x_lab = "reference DBH (cm)" # latex2exp::TeX("reference area ($m^2$)")
    , y_lab = "predicted DBH (cm)" # latex2exp::TeX("predicted area ($m^2$)")
){
    d <- dta
    # construct the formula: "y_var ~ x_var"
    y_var <- paste0("pred_",which_col)
    x_var <- paste0("ref_",which_col)
    formula_obj <- stats::as.formula(
      paste( 
        y_var
        , "~"
        , x_var 
      )
    )
    lm_temp <- lm(
      formula = formula_obj
      , data = d
    )
    # summary(lm_temp)
    # scales::percent(summary(lm_temp)$r.squared, accuracy = 0.1)
    lmf_temp <- paste0(
      "y = "
      , scales::number(summary(lm_temp)$coefficients[2,1], accuracy = 0.01) #slope
      , "x"
      , ifelse(summary(lm_temp)$coefficients[1,1]<0," - ", " + ")
      , scales::number(abs(summary(lm_temp)$coefficients[1,1]), accuracy = 0.01) #intercept
    )
    # scale limits
    max_val <- max( 
      max(d[[y_var]],na.rm=T)
      , max(d[[x_var]],na.rm=T)
    )
    
    # annotations
    if( any( stringr::str_equal(names(d), paste0("diff_",which_col, "_rmse")) ) ){
    rmse_val <- d %>% 
      dplyr::slice(1) %>% 
      dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(paste0("diff_",which_col, "_rmse"))) %>% 
      dplyr::pull(1) %>% 
      unique()
      rmse_val <- dplyr::case_when(
        stringr::str_ends(which_col,"_cm") ~ scales::number(rmse_val, accuracy = 0.1, suffix = " (cm)")
        , stringr::str_ends(which_col,"_m") ~ scales::number(rmse_val, accuracy = 0.01, suffix = " (m)")
        , stringr::str_ends(which_col,"_m2") ~ scales::number(rmse_val, accuracy = 0.1, suffix = " (m²)")
        , stringr::str_ends(which_col,"_pct") ~ scales::percent(rmse_val, accuracy = 0.1)
        , T ~ scales::number(rmse_val, accuracy = 0.1)
      )
    }else{rmse_val <- NA}
    if( any( stringr::str_equal(names(d), paste0("pct_diff_",which_col, "_mape")) ) ){
      mape_val <- d %>% 
        dplyr::slice(1) %>% 
        dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(paste0("pct_diff_",which_col, "_mape"))) %>% 
        dplyr::pull(1) %>% 
        unique() %>% 
        scales::percent(accuracy = 0.1)
    }else{mape_val <- NA}
    if( any( stringr::str_equal(names(d), paste0("diff_",which_col, "_mean")) ) ){
      me_val <- d %>% 
        dplyr::slice(1) %>% 
        dplyr::select(dplyr::all_of(paste0("diff_",which_col, "_mean"))) %>% 
        dplyr::pull(1) %>% 
        unique()
        me_val <- dplyr::case_when(
          stringr::str_ends(which_col,"_cm") ~ scales::number(me_val, accuracy = 0.1, suffix = " (cm)")
          , stringr::str_ends(which_col,"_m") ~ scales::number(me_val, accuracy = 0.01, suffix = " (m)")
          , stringr::str_ends(which_col,"_m2") ~ scales::number(me_val, accuracy = 0.1, suffix = " (m²)")
          , stringr::str_ends(which_col,"_pct") ~ scales::percent(me_val, accuracy = 0.1)
          , T ~ scales::number(me_val, accuracy = 0.1)
        )
    }else{me_val <- NA}
    
    # plot
    p <- d %>% 
      ggplot2::ggplot(mapping = ggplot2::aes(y = .data[[y_var]], x = .data[[x_var]])) +
      ggplot2::geom_abline(color = "gray33", lwd = 1) +
      ggplot2::geom_point(color = "brown", alpha = 0.7, size = 0.9) +
      ggplot2::geom_smooth(lwd = 0.8, method = "lm", se=F, color = "gray", linetype = "dashed") +
      ggplot2::annotate(
        "text", 
        x = -Inf, y = Inf # Top Left
        , label = 
          # paste0(
          #   "R^2 == "
          #   , scales::number((summary(lm_temp)$r.squared)*100, accuracy = 0.1)
          #   , "*'%'"
          # )
          paste0(
            "RMSE = ", rmse_val
            , ifelse(!is.na(mape_val), paste0("\nMAPE = ", mape_val),"")
            , ifelse(!is.na(me_val), paste0("\nME = ", me_val),"")
            , "\n"
            , lmf_temp
            , "\n R² = "
            # , scales::number((summary(lm_temp)$r.squared)*100, accuracy = 0.1, suffix = "%")
            , scales::number((summary(lm_temp)$r.squared), accuracy = 0.01)
          )
        , hjust = -0.1, vjust = 1.1
        , parse = F
        , size = 3
      ) +
      # ggplot2::facet_wrap(
      #   facets = dplyr::vars(dataset_factor), scales = "free", axis.labels = "all"
      #   # , labeller = ggplot2::labeller(class = ggplot2::label_wrap_gen(width = 10))
      # ) +
      ggplot2::labs(
        x = x_lab
        , y = y_lab
        # , color = "image-field\ndiameter diff."
        # , subtitle = latex2exp::TeX("bulk volume ($m^3$) comparison")
        # , subtitle = paste("Predicted", title_lab,"versus Reference", title_lab)
      ) +
      ggplot2::theme_light() +
      ggplot2::theme(
        strip.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 10, face = "bold", color = "black")
      ) 
    if(stringr::str_ends(which_col,"_pct")){
      p + 
        ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, max_val ), labels = scales::percent, expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0,0.1))) +
        ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, max_val ), labels = scales::percent, expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0,0.1)))
    }else{
      return(
        p + 
        ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(limits = c(0, max_val ), expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0,0.1))) +
        ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, max_val ), expand = ggplot2::expansion(mult = c(0,0.1)))
      )
    }
  }
# get the plottttttttttttttttttt
dbh_plt_temp <-
  plt_scatter_fn(dta_temp, which_col = "dbh_cm", title_lab = "DBH", x_lab = "reference DBH (cm)", y_lab = "predicted DBH (cm)")
# dbh_plt_temp

# get the plottttttttttttttttttt
ht_plt_temp <-
  plt_scatter_fn(dta_temp, which_col = "tree_height_m", title_lab = "Height", x_lab = "reference Height (m)", y_lab = "predicted Height (m)")
# ht_plt_temp

DBH: predicted vs reference

dbh_plt_temp

yikes. see comments on DBH above

height: predicted vs reference

ht_plt_temp

UAS-predicted height is highly accurate compared to field-measured values, a finding supported by many prior studies.

full distribution of predicted and reference values irrespective of correct or incorrect prediction match

ground_truth_prediction_match_ans %>%
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>%
  dplyr::select(
    ref_tree_height_m, pred_tree_height_m
    , ref_dbh_cm, pred_dbh_cm
  ) %>% 
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = dplyr::everything()
    , names_to = "metric"
    , values_to = "value"
    , values_drop_na = T
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    which_data = dplyr::case_when(
        stringr::str_starts(metric,"field_") | stringr::str_starts(metric,"ref_") ~ "field"
        , stringr::str_starts(metric,"pred_") ~ "prediction"
        , T ~ "error"
      ) %>% 
      ordered()
    , tree_metric = metric %>% 
      stringr::str_remove("(_rmse|_rrmse|_mean|_mape)$") %>% 
      stringr::str_extract("(height|dbh)") %>% 
      factor(
        ordered = T
        , levels = c(
          "height"
          , "dbh"
        )
        , labels = c(
          "Height (m)"
          , "DBH (cm)"
        )
      )
  ) %>% 
  # dplyr::glimpse()
# plot dist
  ggplot2::ggplot(mapping = ggplot2::aes(x = value, color = which_data, fill = which_data)) +
  ggplot2::geom_density(mapping = ggplot2::aes(y=ggplot2::after_stat(scaled)), alpha = 0.7) +
  ggplot2::facet_grid(
    cols = dplyr::vars(tree_metric)
    , scales = "free_x", axes = "all_x"
    , switch = "y"
  ) +
  harrypotter::scale_color_hp_d(option = "hermionegranger") +
  harrypotter::scale_fill_hp_d(option = "hermionegranger") +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(NULL,breaks=NULL) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks=scales::breaks_extended(n=6)) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    color="",fill="",x=""
    # , subtitle = "comparison of height and DBH distributions"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_light() +
  ggplot2::theme(
    legend.position = "top"
    , strip.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(size = 9, color = "black", face = "bold")
    # , axis.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(size = 6)
    , panel.grid.major.y = ggplot2::element_blank()
    , panel.grid.minor.y = ggplot2::element_blank()
  )

let’s shift our focus to relative error (MAPE) while providing absolute context (RMSE and ME) as text or secondary metrics to look into the penalty we face due to inaccuracies in DBH for small stems. for example, a 3 cm error on a 6 cm small tree is a 50% relative error while that same 3 cm error on a 30 cm merchantable tree is a minor 10% error.

let’s look into the DBH prediction error over the range of tree sizes and use LOESS smoothing to highlight the trend in relative prediction error

# Data trends (dashed) are visualized using a LOESS (locally weighted scatterplot smoothing) regression. 
# A span of 1.0 was utilized to include 100% of the observations in each local neighborhood, 
# and the local polynomial degree was restricted to 1 (local linear regression) to prevent overfitting and boundary inflation at the data extremes.

# The dashed line represents a locally estimated scatterplot smoothing (LOESS) trend line
# , calculated with a smoothing parameter (span) of 1.0 and a local linear polynomial fit (degree = 1).
ground_truth_prediction_match_ans %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    # swap the error calc
    pct_diff_dbh_cm = (pred_dbh_cm-ref_dbh_cm)/ref_dbh_cm
  ) %>%
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>%
  dplyr::filter(match_grp %in% c("true positive")) %>% 
  # dplyr::glimpse()
  ggplot2::ggplot(
    mapping = ggplot2::aes(y = pct_diff_dbh_cm, x = ref_dbh_cm)
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray33", lwd = 1) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(color = "brown", alpha = 0.7, size = 0.7) +
  ggplot2::geom_smooth(
    method = "loess", se=F
    , span = 1 # 0.75 = default
    , method.args = list(degree = 1)
    , lwd = 1, color = "gray", linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  ggplot2::annotate(
    "text"
    , x = Inf
    # , y = 0.045
    , y = Inf
    , label = "prediction > reference"
    , hjust = 1.1
    , vjust = 1.2
    , parse = F
    , size = 2.5
  ) +
  ggplot2::annotate(
    "text"
    , x = Inf
    # , y = -0.04
    , y = -Inf
    , label = "prediction < reference"
    , hjust = 1.1
    , vjust = -0.4
    , parse = F
    , size = 2.5
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::breaks_extended(n=8)) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(-1,1)
    # breaks = seq(-1,1,by=0.2)
    , breaks = scales::breaks_extended(n = 8)
    , labels = scales::percent
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = "field reference DBH (cm)", y = "relative DBH error"
    , subtitle = "relative DBH error (%) by reference tree DBH"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_light()

that’s neat

do it for height too

# Data trends (dashed) are visualized using a LOESS (locally weighted scatterplot smoothing) regression. 
# A span of 1.0 was utilized to include 100% of the observations in each local neighborhood, 
# and the local polynomial degree was restricted to 1 (local linear regression) to prevent overfitting and boundary inflation at the data extremes.

# The dashed line represents a locally estimated scatterplot smoothing (LOESS) trend line
# , calculated with a smoothing parameter (span) of 1.0 and a local linear polynomial fit (degree = 1).
ground_truth_prediction_match_ans %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    # swap the error calc
    pct_diff_tree_height_m = (pred_tree_height_m-ref_tree_height_m)/ref_tree_height_m
  ) %>%
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>%
  dplyr::filter(match_grp %in% c("true positive")) %>% 
  # dplyr::glimpse()
  ggplot2::ggplot(
    mapping = ggplot2::aes(y = pct_diff_tree_height_m, x = ref_tree_height_m)
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray33", lwd = 1) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(color = "brown", alpha = 0.7, size = 0.7) +
  ggplot2::geom_smooth(
    method = "loess", se=F
    , span = 1 # 0.75 = default
    , method.args = list(degree = 1)
    , lwd = 1, color = "gray", linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  ggplot2::annotate(
    "text"
    , x = Inf
    # , y = 0.045
    , y = Inf
    , label = "prediction > reference"
    , hjust = 1.1
    , vjust = 1.2
    , parse = F
    , size = 2.5
  ) +
  ggplot2::annotate(
    "text"
    , x = Inf
    # , y = -0.04
    , y = -Inf
    , label = "prediction < reference"
    , hjust = 1.1
    , vjust = -0.4
    , parse = F
    , size = 2.5
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::breaks_extended(n=8)) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(-1,1)
    # breaks = seq(-1,1,by=0.2)
    , breaks = scales::breaks_extended(n = 8)
    , labels = scales::percent
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = "field reference height (m)", y = "relative height error"
    , subtitle = "relative height error (%) by reference tree height"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_light()

that is also neat

4.8 Stand-Level Accuracy Results

In addition to the tree-level accuracy metrics (e.g. height RMSE and MAPE), we can also calculate stand-level accuracy metrics by aggregating estimates from the reference (ground truth) and predicted trees irrespective of whether or not they were matched (i.e. true positive) during instance matching.

for this data, “stand-level” refers to the combined area covered by all field plots

# uas trees in plots
pred_trees_temp <-
  treetops_sf %>% 
  sf::st_intersection(
    validation_plots_polygons %>% 
      dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
      dplyr::summarise(
        area_m2 = sum(plot_area_m2)
        , n_plots = dplyr::n()
        , geometry = sf::st_union(.)
      ) %>% 
      sf::st_transform(terra::crs(treetops_sf))
  ) %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry()
pred_stand_temp <- 
  pred_trees_temp %>% 
  dplyr::group_by(area_m2, n_plots) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(
    n_trees = dplyr::n()
    , basal_area_m2 = sum(basal_area_m2)
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    area_ha = (area_m2/10000)
    , basal_area_m2_per_ha = basal_area_m2/area_ha
    , trees_per_ha = n_trees/area_ha
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::select(-area_m2)
# dplyr::glimpse(pred_stand_temp)
ref_stand_temp <-
  validation_trees %>% 
  sf::st_transform(terra::crs(treetops_sf)) %>% 
  sf::st_intersection(
    validation_plots_polygons %>% 
      dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
      dplyr::summarise(
        area_m2 = sum(plot_area_m2)
        , n_plots = dplyr::n()
        , geometry = sf::st_union(.)
      ) %>% 
      sf::st_transform(terra::crs(treetops_sf))
  ) %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(basal_area_m2 = pi * dbh_cm^2 / (4*10000)) %>% 
  dplyr::group_by(area_m2, n_plots) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(
    n_trees = dplyr::n()
    , basal_area_m2 = sum(basal_area_m2)
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    area_ha = (area_m2/10000)
    , basal_area_m2_per_ha = basal_area_m2/area_ha
    , trees_per_ha = n_trees/area_ha
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::select(-area_m2)
# dplyr::glimpse(ref_stand_temp)
stand_temp <- 
  dplyr::inner_join(
    ref_stand_temp %>% dplyr::rename_with(.cols = -c(area_ha,n_plots), ~paste0("ref_",.x,recycle0 = T))
    , pred_stand_temp %>% dplyr::rename_with(.cols = -c(area_ha,n_plots), ~paste0("pred_",.x,recycle0 = T))
    , by = dplyr::join_by(area_ha,n_plots)
  ) %>% 
  # comps not done in agg_ground_truth_match()
  dplyr::mutate(
    # 'diff_' columns are calculated as the predicted value minus the actual value
    diff_trees_per_ha = pred_trees_per_ha-ref_trees_per_ha
    , diff_basal_area_m2 = pred_basal_area_m2-ref_basal_area_m2
    , diff_basal_area_m2_per_ha = pred_basal_area_m2_per_ha-ref_basal_area_m2_per_ha
    # 'abs_diff_' columns are calculated as the predicted value minus the actual value
    , abs_diff_trees_per_ha = abs(diff_trees_per_ha)
    , abs_diff_basal_area_m2 = abs(diff_basal_area_m2)
    , abs_diff_basal_area_m2_per_ha = abs(diff_basal_area_m2_per_ha)
    # 'pct_diff_' columns are calculated as the predicted value minus the actual value divided by the actual value
    , pct_diff_trees_per_ha = (pred_trees_per_ha-ref_trees_per_ha)/ref_trees_per_ha
    , pct_diff_basal_area_m2 = (pred_basal_area_m2-ref_basal_area_m2)/ref_basal_area_m2
    , pct_diff_basal_area_m2_per_ha = (pred_basal_area_m2_per_ha-ref_basal_area_m2_per_ha)/ref_basal_area_m2_per_ha
  )

let’s table the stand-level measurement error

stand_temp %>% 
  dplyr::select(
    area_ha,n_plots
    # detection
    , ref_n_trees
    , pred_n_trees
    # physical measurement
    , tidyselect::ends_with("_basal_area_m2_per_ha")
    , tidyselect::ends_with("_trees_per_ha")
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::select(!tidyselect::starts_with("abs_diff_")) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    dplyr::across(
      .cols = c(
        tidyselect::starts_with("pct_diff_")
        , tidyselect::ends_with("_rate")
      )
      , .fn = ~ scales::percent(.x, accuracy = .1)
    )
    , dplyr::across(
      .cols = c(n_plots,tidyselect::ends_with("_trees"))
      , .fn = ~ scales::comma(.x, accuracy = 1)
    )
    , dplyr::across(
      .cols = dplyr::where(is.numeric)
      , .fn = ~ scales::comma(.x, accuracy = 0.1)
    )
    # # one column for diff/pct
    , diff_basal_area_m2_per_ha = paste0(diff_basal_area_m2_per_ha, "<br>(", pct_diff_basal_area_m2_per_ha, ")")
    , diff_trees_per_ha = paste0(diff_trees_per_ha, "<br>(", pct_diff_trees_per_ha, ")")
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::select(
    !tidyselect::starts_with("pct_diff_")
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::relocate(n_plots) %>% 
  # dplyr::glimpse()
  
  kableExtra::kbl(
    caption = "Stand-Level Accuracy"
    , col.names = c(
      "# plots","field-measured area (ha)"
      , "reference", "predicted"
      , rep(c("reference","predicted","difference"), times = 2)
    )
    , escape = F
    # , digits = 2
  ) %>% 
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 11.5) %>% 
  kableExtra::add_header_above(
    c(
      " "=2
      , "# Trees" = 2
      # , "Area" = 3
      , "Basal Area<br />m<sup>2</sup> ha<sup>-1</sup>" = 3
      , "Trees<br />ha<sup>-1</sup>" = 3
    )
    , escape = F
  ) %>% 
  kableExtra::column_spec( c(2,seq(4,10,by=3) ), border_right = TRUE, include_thead = TRUE) %>% 
  kableExtra::collapse_rows(columns = c(1), valign = "top")
Table 4.6: Stand-Level Accuracy
# Trees

Basal Area
m2 ha-1

Trees
ha-1
# plots field-measured area (ha) reference predicted reference predicted difference reference predicted difference
176 5.5 1,505 1,498 7.6 15.9 8.3
(108.9%) 		272.2 270.9 -1.3
(-0.5%)

Should we even be calculating basal area for pinyon-juniper systems? Traditional silvicultural metrics like basal area and volume are not particularly useful for managing these woodlands because the trees/shrubs feature highly irregular, multi-stemmed growth forms. Furthermore, field inventories like FIA measure diameter at root collar (DRC) instead of standard diameter at breast height (DBH) and then convert those metrics using regional allometric equations. Because of this, it is probably inappropriate for us to quantify the accuracy of UAS-predicted DBH, which is modeled using a height-to-DBH equation from local FIA data, against field-calculated DBH values derived from converted DRC measurements. Instead, modern management evaluations in these pinyon-juniper systems prioritize tree density measurements and canopy clump configurations to analyze structural and spatial patterns. This focus on horizontal canopy architecture, tracking trees per hectare and interspace connectivity, provides a much more direct and ecologically meaningful way to evaluate ecosystem health and fire risk across these arid landscapes.

Let’s shift our focus to evaluating UAS-predicted canopy cover compared to field-measured values. Recall that to obtain field-measured canopy cover, we averaged the field-recorded horizontal crown length and width to approximate a circular diameter, used that diameter to calculate a 2D circular crown area for each tree, and determined the proportion of each plot covered by these assumed circular crowns. For each plot, we’ll compare this proportion to the canopy cover predicted by the UAS using trees whose tree-top points fall within the plot boundaries irrespective of if a tree was matched as a true positive in our instance matching evaluation. Requiring the tree-top point to fall within the plot mimics the field technique of only measuring stems located inside the plot boundaries. The main caveats attached to this canopy cover accuracy evaluation are that measuring crown dimensions accurately using manual ground techniques is notoriously difficult, and the simple circular shape assumption rarely matches the irregular crown morphologies found in the real-world (Rautiainen et al. 2008; Gallardo-Salazar & Pompa-García 2020; Ren et al. 2024).

# read crowns
pred_crowns_temp <- 
  cloud2trees:::search_dir_final_detected(dir = c2t_process_dir)[["crowns_flist"]] %>% 
  purrr::map(
    \(x)
    crowns_sf <- sf::st_read(
      dsn = x, quiet = TRUE
      , query = 
        sprintf(
          "SELECT * FROM %s WHERE treeID IN (%s)"
          , basename(x) %>% stringr::str_remove("^.*/") %>% stringr::str_remove("\\.[^.]+$")
          , stringr::str_c("'", unique(pred_trees_temp$treeID), "'", collapse = ", ")
        )
    ) %>% 
    cloud2trees::simplify_multipolygon_crowns()
  ) %>% 
  purrr::list_rbind()
# pred_crowns_temp %>% dplyr::glimpse()
# pred_trees_temp %>% nrow()
# pred_crowns_temp %>% nrow()
# attach predicted cc to plot data
validation_plots_polygons <- plot_crowns_cc_fn(
    plots_sf = validation_plots_polygons
    , crowns_sf = pred_crowns_temp
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::rename_with(
    .cols = tidyselect::starts_with("canopy_")
    , ~ paste0("pred_",.x,recycle0 = T)
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    diff_canopy_cov_pct = pred_canopy_cov_pct-ref_canopy_cov_pct
    , diff_canopy_cov_area_m2 = pred_canopy_cov_area_m2-ref_canopy_cov_area_m2
  )

# validation_plots_polygons %>% dplyr::glimpse()

quickly, we’ll look at a sample of the UAS-detected crowns within the plot locations and the percent canopy cover for those plots

plts_temp <- 
  1:nrow(validation_plots_polygons) %>% 
  sample(16) %>% 
  purrr::map(
    \(x)
    validation_plots_polygons %>% 
      dplyr::slice(x) %>% 
      dplyr::mutate(
        lab = paste0(
          "plot: ", plot
          , "\ncanopy cover = "
          , scales::percent(pred_canopy_cov_pct,accuracy=0.1)
        )
      ) %>% 
      ggplot2::ggplot() +
      ggplot2::geom_sf(color = "gray", fill = "gray") +
      ggplot2::geom_sf(
        data = 
          validation_plots_polygons %>% 
            dplyr::slice(x) %>% 
            sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
            sf::st_set_geometry("pred_canopy_cov_geom") %>% 
            sf::st_set_crs(sf::st_crs(validation_plots_polygons))
        , color = "forestgreen", fill = "forestgreen"
      ) +
      ggplot2::facet_wrap(dplyr::vars(lab)) + 
      ggplot2::theme_light() +
      ggplot2::theme(
        strip.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(color = "black", face = "bold", size = 7)
        , panel.grid = ggplot2::element_blank()
        , axis.text = ggplot2::element_blank()
        , axis.title = ggplot2::element_blank()
      )
  )
patchwork::wrap_plots(plts_temp, ncol = 4)

let’s check the relationship between the UAS and reference field plot-level canopy cover measurements

cc_agg_temp <- validation_plots_polygons %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
  dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(
    sq = sum(diff_canopy_cov_pct^2, na.rm = T)
    , mean = mean(diff_canopy_cov_pct, na.rm = T)
    , sumabs = sum(abs(diff_canopy_cov_pct), na.rm = T)
    , nomiss = sum(!is.na(diff_canopy_cov_pct))
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    rmse = dplyr::case_when(
      dplyr::coalesce(nomiss,0)==0 ~ as.numeric(NA)
      , T ~ sqrt(sq/nomiss)
    )
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::select(mean,rmse) %>% 
  dplyr::rename_with(
    ~paste0("diff_canopy_cov_pct_",.x, recycle0 = T)
  ) 
# get the plottttttttttttttttttt
validation_plots_polygons %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
  dplyr::bind_cols(
    cc_agg_temp    
  ) %>% 
  plt_scatter_fn(which_col = "canopy_cov_pct", title_lab = "Canopy Cover", x_lab = "reference Canopy Cover (%)", y_lab = "predicted Canopy Cover (%)")

The plot-level aggregation comparing the canopy cover of the field method to the UAS method yielded an RMSE of 9.2% and a mean error (ME) of 5.6%. These values mean that the average UAS prediction of plot-level canopy cover is off by about ±9.2 percentage points from the field-measured value which is generally considered highly accurate and operationally acceptable. The positive ME of 5.6% means that, on average across all plots, the UAS method is systematically overpredicting canopy cover by 5.6 percentage points compared to the field data.

we can table the canopy cover measurements aggregated over all field plots

# comps not done in agg_ground_truth_match()
validation_plots_polygons %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
  dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(
    n_plots = sum(!is.na(diff_canopy_cov_pct))
    , plot_area_ha = sum(plot_area_m2)/10000
    , dplyr::across(
      .cols = c(ref_canopy_cov_pct,pred_canopy_cov_pct,diff_canopy_cov_pct)
      , mean
    )
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    # pct_diff_canopy_cov_pct = (pred_canopy_cov_pct-ref_canopy_cov_pct)/ref_canopy_cov_pct
    dplyr::across(c(tidyselect::ends_with("_pct")),~scales::percent(.x,accuracy=0.1))
    , dplyr::across(c(plot_area_ha),~scales::number(.x,accuracy=0.1))
    , dplyr::across(dplyr::where(is.numeric),~scales::number(.x,accuracy=1))
  ) %>% 
  kableExtra::kbl(
    caption = "Canopy Cover Across all Measured Plots"
    , col.names = c(
      "# plots", "field-measured area (ha)"
      , rep(c("reference","predicted","difference"), times = 1)
    )
    , escape = F
    # , digits = 2
  ) %>% 
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 11.5) %>% 
  kableExtra::add_header_above(
    c(
      " "=2
      , "Canopy Cover (%)" = 3
    )
    , escape = F
  ) %>% 
  kableExtra::column_spec( c(2,seq(2,5,by=3) ), border_right = TRUE, include_thead = TRUE) %>% 
  kableExtra::collapse_rows(columns = c(1), valign = "top")
Table 4.7: Canopy Cover Across all Measured Plots
Canopy Cover (%)
# plots field-measured area (ha) reference predicted difference
176 5.5 20.8% 26.4% 5.6%

let’s check that out by treatment type

validation_plots_polygons %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
  dplyr::group_by(trtmnt_type) %>% 
  dplyr::summarise(
    n_plots = sum(!is.na(diff_canopy_cov_pct))
    , plot_area_ha = sum(plot_area_m2)/10000
    , dplyr::across(
      .cols = c(n_trees,ref_canopy_cov_pct,pred_canopy_cov_pct,diff_canopy_cov_pct)
      , mean
    )
  ) %>% 
  dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    # pct_diff_canopy_cov_pct = (pred_canopy_cov_pct-ref_canopy_cov_pct)/ref_canopy_cov_pct
    dplyr::across(c(tidyselect::ends_with("_pct")),~scales::percent(.x,accuracy=0.1))
    , dplyr::across(c(plot_area_ha),~scales::number(.x,accuracy=0.1))
    , dplyr::across(dplyr::where(is.numeric),~scales::number(.x,accuracy=1))
  ) %>% 
  kableExtra::kbl(
    caption = "Canopy Cover Across all Measured Plots"
    , col.names = c(
      "treatment","# plots", "field-measured area (ha)"
      , "mean<br /># trees"
      , rep(c("reference","predicted","difference"), times = 1)
    )
    , escape = F
    # , digits = 2
  ) %>% 
  kableExtra::kable_styling(font_size = 11.5) %>% 
  kableExtra::add_header_above(
    c(
      " "=4
      , "Canopy Cover (%)" = 3
    )
    , escape = F
  ) %>% 
  kableExtra::column_spec( c(3,4,7), border_right = TRUE, include_thead = TRUE) %>% 
  kableExtra::collapse_rows(columns = c(1), valign = "top")
Table 4.8: Canopy Cover Across all Measured Plots
Canopy Cover (%)
treatment # plots field-measured area (ha) mean
# trees 		reference predicted difference
control 45 1.4 16 31.5% 41.2% 9.6%
heterogenous 46 1.4 11 24.7% 30.3% 5.6%
uniform- high 38 1.2 3 10.2% 12.6% 2.4%
uniform- low 47 1.5 5 15.4% 19.7% 4.3%

Note that the overprediction of canopy cover by the UAS method compared to the field measurement increases as the tree density increases. Let’s look at that trend

# Data trends (dashed) are visualized using a LOESS (locally weighted scatterplot smoothing) regression. 
# A span of 1.0 was utilized to include 100% of the observations in each local neighborhood, 
# and the local polynomial degree was restricted to 1 (local linear regression) to prevent overfitting and boundary inflation at the data extremes.

# The dashed line represents a locally estimated scatterplot smoothing (LOESS) trend line
# , calculated with a smoothing parameter (span) of 1.0 and a local linear polynomial fit (degree = 1).
validation_plots_polygons %>%
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
  # dplyr::glimpse()
  ggplot2::ggplot(
    mapping = ggplot2::aes(y = diff_canopy_cov_pct, x = trees_per_ha)
  ) + 
  ggplot2::geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray33", lwd = 1) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(color = "brown", alpha = 0.7, size = 0.7) +
  ggplot2::geom_smooth(
    method = "loess", se=F
    , span = 1 # 0.75 = default
    , method.args = list(degree = 1)
    , lwd = 1, color = "gray", linetype = "dashed"
  ) +
  ggplot2::annotate(
    "text"
    , x = Inf
    # , y = 0.045
    , y = Inf
    , label = "prediction > reference"
    , hjust = 1.1
    , vjust = 1.2
    , parse = F
    , size = 2.5
  ) +
  ggplot2::annotate(
    "text"
    , x = Inf
    # , y = -0.04
    , y = -Inf
    , label = "prediction < reference"
    , hjust = 1.1
    , vjust = -0.4
    , parse = F
    , size = 2.5
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::breaks_extended(n=8)) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(
    limits = c(-1,1)
    # breaks = seq(-1,1,by=0.2)
    , breaks = scales::breaks_extended(n = 8)
    , labels = scales::percent
  ) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x = "field reference TPH", y = "Canopy Cover difference"
    , subtitle = "Canopy Cover difference by reference TPH"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_light()

The higher canopy cover predictions by the UAS method compared to the field method is a logical outcome of how crown boundaries are defined by each method. The UAS approach delineates crowns from an aerial perspective and utilizes a segmentation routine that prevents any two crowns from overlapping. Conversely, the field method assumes a perfectly circular crown extending symmetrically from the tree stem. In dense stands, this circular geometry causes field-calculated crowns to overlap in 2D space which may artificially reduce the total canopy cover. In reality, tree branches grow asymmetrically. For example, extending away from competitors to capture available sunlight. The UAS captures this irregular, outward branch expansion which may explain why it results in larger (and more realistic) canopy cover measurements than the field-based method.

let’s check out a visual representation of that phenomenon by looking as some example plots

# harrypotter::hp(n=2, option="hermionegranger") %>% scales::show_col()
terra_plt_fn_temp <- function(rgb, rgb_square, plot_poly, ref_pt, ref_poly, pred_poly, plot_title="") {
  grDevices::dev.new()
  grDevices::dev.control("enable")
  
  terra::plotRGB(rgb)
  terra::plot(rgb_square, add = T, border = "white", col = NA, lwd = 2)
  terra::plot(terra::vect(plot_poly %>% sf::st_transform(terra::crs(rgb)) ), add = T, border = "white", col = NA, lwd = 0.8)
  terra::plot(terra::vect(ref_pt %>% sf::st_transform(terra::crs(rgb)) ), add = T, col = harrypotter::hp(n=2, option="hermionegranger")[1])
  terra::plot(terra::vect(ref_poly %>% sf::st_transform(terra::crs(rgb)) ), add = T, border = harrypotter::hp(n=2, option="hermionegranger")[1], col = NA, lwd = 1.4)
  terra::plot(terra::vect(pred_poly %>% sf::st_transform(terra::crs(rgb)) ), add = T, border = harrypotter::hp(n=2, option="hermionegranger")[2], col = NA, lwd = 1.4)
  # graphics::title(main = plot_title, cex.main = 0, col.main = "white", font.main = 2) 
  
  p <- grDevices::recordPlot()
  grDevices::dev.off()
  return(p)
}

# for a single record in the validation plot data
plt_validation_plots_polygons_temp <- function(x){
    # plot
    plot_temp <- validation_plots_polygons %>% dplyr::slice(x)
    # sq
    rgb_sq_temp <- plot_temp %>% 
      sf::st_buffer(1.5) %>% 
      sf::st_bbox() %>% 
      sf::st_as_sfc() %>% 
      sf::st_transform(terra::crs(rgb_rast)) %>% 
      terra::vect()
    # rgb
    rgb_temp <- rgb_rast %>% 
       terra::crop(
          rgb_sq_temp
        , mask=T
      )
    # ref pts
    ref_pt_temp <- validation_trees %>% dplyr::filter(plot==plot_temp$plot)
    # ref polys
    ref_poly_temp <- 
      ref_pt_temp %>% 
      dplyr::mutate(crown_radius_m=crown_diam_m/2) %>% 
      sf::st_buffer(.$crown_radius_m)
    # pred polys
    pred_poly_temp <- 
      pred_crowns_temp %>% 
        dplyr::inner_join(
          treetops_sf %>% 
            sf::st_intersection(plot_temp %>% sf::st_transform(sf::st_crs(treetops_sf)) ) %>% 
            sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
            dplyr::select(treeID)
          , by = "treeID"
        )
    # plt
    plt <- terra_plt_fn_temp(
      rgb = rgb_temp 
      , rgb_square = rgb_sq_temp 
      , plot_poly = plot_temp 
      , ref_pt = ref_pt_temp 
      , ref_poly = ref_poly_temp 
      , pred_poly = pred_poly_temp 
      , plot_title = paste0("plot: ",plot_temp$plot)
    )
    return(plt)
  }

# get individual plots
set.seed(44)
plts_temp <- 
  1:nrow(validation_plots_polygons) %>% 
  sample(9) %>% 
  purrr::map(plt_validation_plots_polygons_temp)

# plts_temp[[1]]
# plts_temp[[2]]
# plts_temp[[3]]
# patchwork::wrap_plots(plts_temp, ncol = 3) doesn't work :\

combine_plts_temp <- function(plot_list) {
  n_plots <- length(plot_list)
  n_cols <- min(n_plots, 3)
  n_rows <- ceiling(n_plots / n_cols)
  
  grob_list <- lapply(plot_list, function(p) {
    cowplot::as_grob(p)
  })
  
  # grDevices::dev.new()
  cowplot::plot_grid(
    plotlist = grob_list
    , ncol = n_cols
    , nrow = n_rows
  )  
  # graphics::par(mfrow = c(n_rows, n_cols), mar = c(1, 1, 2, 1))
  #   for (i in seq_len(n_plots)) {
  #   grDevices::replayPlot(plot_list[[i]])
  # }
}

plt_temp <- combine_plts_temp(plts_temp)
cowplot::save_plot(
  filename = "../data/field_uas_crown_comp.png"
  , plot = plt_temp 
  , base_width = 10
  , base_height = 10
  , ncol = 1
  , nrow = 1
  , dpi = 300
  , bg = "white"
)
print(plt_temp)

interesting. we see that the UAS crowns (orange) generally align with the green areas that appear to be trees from the aerial perspective and,the individual crowns segmented using the UAS method do not overlap. By contrast, the field-measured stems and the assumed circular crowns (red) overlap with each other and are not always spatially aligned with the green areas in the aerial imagery. This slight misalignment is the result of variations in the location recording systems used on the ground and the UAS system in addition to the stems of trees not being positioned directly under the crown due to real-world tree lean. We can also see a few examples of field-measured tree stems that are very near each other so that the simulated circular crowns have high or even complete overlap.

We can move beyond summary canopy cover percentages and evaluate the spatial overlap of the crown footprints using the Intersection over Union (IoU) metric. The IoU index provides a spatial assessment by measuring the geographic alignment between the UAS crown polygons and circular field crowns. To quantify the spatial agreement between your UAS-delineated crowns and the field-calculated circular crowns, IoU is calculated by dividing the area of overlap between the two crown layers by the total combined area of both layers:

\[ \text{IoU}=\frac{\text{Area of Intersection}}{\text{Area of Union}}=\frac{|A\cap B|}{|A\cup B|} \]

where \(A\) represents the spatial footprint of the field-calculated circular crowns within a plot and \(B\) represents the spatial footprint of the UAS-segmented crown polygons within the same plot.

# validation_plots_polygons %>% dplyr::glimpse()
iou_temp <-
  1:nrow(validation_plots_polygons) %>% 
  purrr::map(function(x){
    # plot
    plot_temp <- validation_plots_polygons %>% dplyr::slice(x)
    # ref pts
    ref_pt_temp <- validation_trees %>% dplyr::filter(plot==plot_temp$plot)
    # ref polys
    ref_poly_temp <- 
      ref_pt_temp %>% 
      dplyr::mutate(crown_radius_m=crown_diam_m/2) %>% 
      sf::st_buffer(.$crown_radius_m) %>% 
      sf::st_transform(sf::st_crs(treetops_sf)) %>% 
      sf::st_union()
    # pred polys
    pred_poly_temp <- 
      pred_crowns_temp %>% 
        dplyr::inner_join(
          treetops_sf %>% 
            sf::st_intersection(plot_temp %>% sf::st_transform(sf::st_crs(treetops_sf)) ) %>% 
            sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
            dplyr::select(treeID)
          , by = "treeID"
        ) %>% 
        sf::st_union()
    u <- sf::st_union(ref_poly_temp,pred_poly_temp)
    i <- sf::st_intersection(ref_poly_temp,pred_poly_temp)
    
    return(
      plot_temp %>% 
        sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
        dplyr::select(plot) %>% 
        dplyr::mutate(
          crown_pred_ref_iou = as.numeric(sf::st_area(i))/as.numeric(sf::st_area(u))
        )
    )
  }) %>% 
  purrr::list_rbind()
# add
validation_plots_polygons <- validation_plots_polygons %>% dplyr::left_join(iou_temp, by = "plot")

what is the distribution of crown IoU values over the measured plots?

validation_plots_polygons %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
# plot dist
  ggplot2::ggplot(mapping = ggplot2::aes(x = crown_pred_ref_iou)) +
  ggplot2::geom_density(
    mapping = ggplot2::aes(y=ggplot2::after_stat(scaled))
    , alpha = 0.8
    , fill = "brown", color = "brown"
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_vline(
    mapping = ggplot2::aes(xintercept = median(validation_plots_polygons$crown_pred_ref_iou,na.rm=T) )
    , linetype = "dashed", lwd = 1, color = "gray33"
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(NULL,breaks=NULL) +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks=scales::breaks_extended(n=8)) +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x="tree crown Intersection over Union (IoU)"
    , subtitle = "spatial alignment between the UAS crown polygons and circular field crowns"
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme_light() +
  ggplot2::theme(
    panel.grid.major.y = ggplot2::element_blank()
    , panel.grid.minor.y = ggplot2::element_blank()
  )

get the IoU summary stats

validation_plots_polygons %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
  dplyr::select(crown_pred_ref_iou) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(dplyr::across(dplyr::everything(),~round(.x,3))) %>% 
  dplyr::rename(`tree crown IoU`=crown_pred_ref_iou) %>% 
  summary() %>% 
  knitr::kable(format = "markdown")
tree crown IoU
Min. :0.0470
1st Qu.:0.3010
Median :0.4565
Mean :0.4354
3rd Qu.:0.5683
Max. :0.7910

let’s check out some examples of high, medium, and low crown overlap plots

set.seed(55)
plts_temp <- 
  validation_plots_polygons %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    pct_crown_pred_ref_iou = dplyr::percent_rank(crown_pred_ref_iou)
    , grp_temp = dplyr::case_when(
      pct_crown_pred_ref_iou <= 0.1 ~ "low IoU"
      , pct_crown_pred_ref_iou >= 0.9 ~ "high IoU"
      , pct_crown_pred_ref_iou > 0.4 & pct_crown_pred_ref_iou < 0.6 ~ "mid IoU"
      , T ~ "other"
    ) %>% 
    ordered(levels=c("low IoU","mid IoU","high IoU"))
    , row_temp = dplyr::row_number()
  ) %>% 
  # dplyr::group_by(grp_temp) %>% 
  # dplyr::summarise(
  #   dplyr::across(
  #     crown_pred_ref_iou
  #     , list(min,max,median,mean)
  #   )
  # )
  dplyr::slice_sample(n=3, by = grp_temp) %>% 
  dplyr::filter(grp_temp != "other") %>% 
  dplyr::group_by(grp_temp) %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(order_temp = dplyr::row_number()) %>% 
  dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
  dplyr::arrange(order_temp,grp_temp) %>% 
  # dplyr::glimpse()
  dplyr::pull(row_temp) %>% 
  purrr::map(plt_validation_plots_polygons_temp)
# plts_temp[[1]]
# plts_temp[[2]]
# plts_temp[[3]]

grobs_temp <- purrr::map(plts_temp, cowplot::as_grob)
# grobs_temp[[1]]
  
plt_temp <- cowplot::plot_grid(
  plotlist = grobs_temp
  , ncol = 3
  , nrow = 3
)
# plt_temp
# ?cowplot::draw_label
tit1_temp <- cowplot::ggdraw() + 
  cowplot::draw_label(
    paste0(
      "IoU: "
      , scales::number(as.numeric(min(validation_plots_polygons$crown_pred_ref_iou)),accuracy = 0.01)
      , " to "
      , scales::number(as.numeric(quantile(validation_plots_polygons$crown_pred_ref_iou,0.1)),accuracy = 0.01)
    )
    , fontface = 'bold', x = 0.5, hjust = 0.5, size = 10
  )
tit2_temp <- cowplot::ggdraw() + 
  cowplot::draw_label(
    paste0(
      "IoU: "
      , scales::number(as.numeric(quantile(validation_plots_polygons$crown_pred_ref_iou,0.4)),accuracy = 0.01)
      , " to "
      , scales::number(as.numeric(quantile(validation_plots_polygons$crown_pred_ref_iou,0.6)),accuracy = 0.01)
    )
    , fontface = 'bold', x = 0.5, hjust = 0.5, size = 10
  )
tit3_temp <- cowplot::ggdraw() + 
  cowplot::draw_label(
    paste0(
      "IoU: "
      , scales::number(as.numeric(quantile(validation_plots_polygons$crown_pred_ref_iou,0.9)),accuracy = 0.01)
      , " to "
      , scales::number(as.numeric(max(validation_plots_polygons$crown_pred_ref_iou)),accuracy = 0.01)
    )
    , fontface = 'bold', x = 0.5, hjust = 0.5, size = 10
  )
plt_tit_temp <- cowplot::plot_grid(tit1_temp, tit2_temp, tit3_temp, ncol = 3)
# plt_tit_temp


fnl_plt_temp <- cowplot::plot_grid(plt_tit_temp, plt_temp, ncol = 1, rel_heights = c(0.05, 1))
# ?cowplot::save_plot
cowplot::save_plot(
  filename = "../data/field_uas_crown_iou_comp.png"
  , plot = fnl_plt_temp 
  , base_width = 9.9
  , base_height = 10
  , ncol = 1
  , nrow = 1
  , dpi = 300
  , bg = "white"
)
print(fnl_plt_temp)

there are certainly some interesting things going on with these visuals…

finally, let’s check out the distribution of crown IoU by treatment type

validation_plots_polygons %>% 
  dplyr::ungroup() %>% 
  sf::st_drop_geometry() %>% 
  dplyr::mutate(
    trtmnt_type = ordered(trtmnt_type)
  ) %>% 
  ggplot2::ggplot(mapping = ggplot2::aes(x=crown_pred_ref_iou,fill=trtmnt_type)) +
  ggplot2::geom_point(mapping = ggplot2::aes(y=0), color = "gray") +
  ggplot2::geom_violin(
    mapping = ggplot2::aes(y = 0)
    , alpha = 0.8, color = NA
  ) +
  ggplot2::geom_boxplot(width = 0.1, fill = NA, outliers = F) +
  ggplot2::facet_grid(
    rows = dplyr::vars(trtmnt_type)
    , scales = "free_y"
    , switch = "y"
    , axes = "all_x"
  ) +
  ggplot2::scale_fill_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  ggplot2::scale_color_brewer(palette = "Dark2") +
  ggplot2::scale_x_continuous(breaks = scales::breaks_extended(n=8)) +
  ggplot2::scale_y_continuous(NULL,breaks=NULL) +
  ggplot2::theme_light() +
  ggplot2::labs(
    x="tree crown Intersection over Union (IoU)"
    , subtitle = "spatial alignment between the UAS crown polygons and circular field crowns"
    , y = ""
  ) +
  ggplot2::theme(
    legend.position = "none"
    , axis.text.y = ggplot2::element_text(size = 7)
    , axis.text.x = ggplot2::element_text(size = 8)
    , strip.text = ggplot2::element_text(size = 10, color = "black", face = "bold")
    , strip.placement = "outside"
  )

treatment intensity seems to have minimal impact on the spatial agreement between the UAS crown polygons and circular field crowns
